Title: High-level semi-synthetic production of the potent antimalarial artemisinin

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CTR3 promoter (copper-repressible) replacing MET3 for ERG9 repression; MET3 promoter in prior strain; GAL induction system used broadly for heterologous pathway genes; GAL80 deletion enabled constitutive GAL-regulated expression
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Engineered strains derived from CEN.PK2-1C/Y337 lineage; strains include Y285, Y301, Y657, Y692, Y973, Y1283, Y1284, Y1368, Y1516
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Downregulate competing sterol synthesis to redirect FPP toward sesquiterpene production. Useful as a flux-control lever in terpene engineering, preferably with tunable repressible promoters (for example CTR3) rather than full knockout to preserve viability. Best deployed together with strong mevalonate-pathway enhancement and full downstream oxidation pathway; excessive repression may impair sterol supply and growth.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Repression of ERG9 reduced competing conversion of farnesyl diphosphate to squalene, enabling higher carbon flux into amorphadiene/artemisinic acid production without loss of function when MET3 was replaced by CTR3.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Promoter replacement, strain comparison in shake flasks and fed-batch fermentation, sesquiterpene titre profiling
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: CuSO4 addition represses CTR3-driven ERG9; methionine represses MET3-driven ERG9 in older design; glucose/ethanol mixed-feed fermentation; early induction/repression conditions
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered expression under copper-repressible promoter in yeast during fermentation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Native yeast sterol-biosynthetic gene; no specific expression pattern reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ERG9 encodes squalene synthase, a competing sink for FPP. CTR3 promoter performed equivalently to MET3 promoter for controlling ERG9 while using cheaper CuSO4 as repressor.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Too much ERG9 repression can compromise sterol biosynthesis and cell fitness; balancing repression is required.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Squalene synthase; prenyltransferase-related sterol biosynthesis enzyme
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ERG9 is a key competing-pathway gene consuming FPP for squalene synthesis. 2. Replacing PMET3 with copper-repressible PCTR3 maintained amorphadiene output while lowering process cost. 3. Controlled ERG9 repression was part of the platform enabling high artemisinic acid titres.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Engineered yeast fermentation broth; artemisinic acid also accumulated in extracellular IPM phase/precipitate
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Artemisinin is a potent antimalarial drug precursor/product used in ACTs

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Strong galactose-regulated promoters on high-copy plasmid in earlier strains; GAL-regulated expression in engineered yeast
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Engineered strains Y337-derived, including Y285 and later strains
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Foundational entry enzyme for heterologous artemisinin precursor production. Must be combined with mevalonate-pathway enhancement and downstream oxidation genes. Useful for microbial sesquiterpene chassis engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies amorphadiene precursor for subsequent oxidation to artemisinic acid; high amorphadiene titre achieved in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast, fermentation titres of amorphadiene, pathway reconstruction
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms the dedicated hydrocarbon precursor to target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction in some strains; fermentation carbon source regime (glucose/ethanol)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered heterologous expression in yeast during production phase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not detailed here; known trichome-associated in Artemisia annua but not discussed in this paper text
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously introduced in strain Y337 and retained in all production strains; codon-optimized synthetic gene used.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No specific tradeoff reported here beyond need for sufficient downstream oxidation capacity.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Committed-step enzyme for pathway entry; not specifically designated rate-limiting in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADS provides the first dedicated step to amorphadiene in yeast. 2. High amorphadiene production established the precursor platform for oxidation steps. 3. Full conversion to artemisinic acid required addition of downstream plant oxidation enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fermentation broth/IPM overlay in engineered yeast
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Artemisinin is an antimalarial endoperoxide used in ACTs

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Strong galactose-regulated promoters on high-copy plasmid in early strains; GAL-regulated expression in later pathway strains
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Y285, Y301, Y657, Y692, Y973, Y1283, Y1284 and related strains
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential P450 oxidation catalyst for artemisinic acid production, but must be balanced with CPR1 and supported by CYB5, ADH1, and ALDH1 to achieve industrial titres and maintain viability. Expression-ratio tuning is critical in metabolic engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Performs sequential oxidations of amorpha-4,11-diene to artemisinic alcohol and further toward artemisinic aldehyde/acid in conjunction with redox partners
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts amorphadiene into oxidized artemisinin precursors; expression alone with CPR1 gave low artemisinic acid titres and impaired viability, whereas full pathway support markedly improved output.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast, metabolite profiling of amorphadiene/artexmisinic alcohol/aldehyde/acid, strain comparisons with and without accessory enzymes
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Initiates oxidation series directly leading to target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction; glucose/ethanol mixed-feed fermentation; oxidative stress context; IPM extractive fermentation improved performance
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered expression in yeast during induced/constitutive production conditions
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not detailed in this paper text
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Expression of CYP71AV1 plus CPR1 was insufficient for high-level artemisinic acid production and caused loss of viability, likely from ROS or product toxicity. Full efficient route required additional CYB5, ADH1, ALDH1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Poor coupling with CPR1 can generate reactive oxygen species and reduce viability; incomplete downstream oxidation leads to intermediate accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Artemisinic alcohol primarily; further oxidized intermediates also produced
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point/bottleneck in oxidation module
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71AV1 enabled conversion of amorphadiene to oxidized intermediates in yeast. 2. CYP71AV1 with CPR1 alone caused viability defects and low artemisinic acid titres. 3. Proper redox balancing and addition of CYB5, ADH1 and ALDH1 unlocked high-level artemisinic acid production.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Initially strong galactose-regulated promoter on high-copy plasmid; later weakened by single-copy genomic integration under GAL3 promoter
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Y285/Y301 high expression; Y657 and descendants low CPR1 expression
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: P450 redox support in artemisinic acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Tune CPR1 downward relative to CYP71AV1 to improve coupling and viability. Best used as a dosage-balanced redox partner rather than maximally overexpressed. Important general lesson for P450 pathway engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers electrons from NADPH to CYP71AV1 to support monooxygenase catalysis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for CYP71AV1 activity, but excessive CPR1 expression impaired cell health; reduced CPR1 improved growth/viability while lowering oxidation rate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Promoter engineering and genomic integration, growth/viability assays, fermentation metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required redox partner for direct formation of oxidized precursors
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction; oxidative stress context during fermentation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered expression; lower single-copy PGAL3 expression in improved strains
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors infer poor P450/reductase coupling and ROS release may explain toxicity. Lower CPR1 preserved total sesquiterpene relatively well but reduced artemisinic acid formation unless CYB5 and dehydrogenases were added.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression associated with reduced viability; underexpression limits total oxidation rate and acid titre.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Reduced CYP71AV1 catalytic cycle / electron transfer support
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point via expression ratio rather than classic catalytic bottleneck
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPR1 is necessary for CYP71AV1 function in yeast. 2. High CPR1 expression correlated with poor viability, likely due to redox imbalance/ROS. 3. Lower integrated CPR1 improved health but required CYB5 and downstream enzymes to restore high artemisinic acid production.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Single chromosomal copy expressed from strong GAL7 promoter
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Coexpress CYB5 with plant P450 systems to improve oxidation efficiency and titre. Valuable accessory factor for industrial P450 pathway optimization, especially when reductase dosage is limited.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cytochrome b5 enhances CYP71AV1-mediated oxidation of amorphadiene, likely by improving electron transfer or catalytic coupling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased artemisinic acid and artemisinic aldehyde production and raised total sesquiterpene output by about 40% in low-CPR1 background.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene discovery from trichome ESTs, heterologous expression in yeast, shake-flask and fermentor metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Enhances direct formation of oxidized precursors leading to target product
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified from Artemisia annua trichome EST library
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Auxiliary support to upstream/midstream oxidation steps
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: EST library from trichomes, NCBI accession 35608
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper identifies CYB5 as a second cytochrome completing an efficient route. It almost doubled aldehyde production in fermentors in low-CPR1 background.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No explicit penalty reported; benefit depends on P450/reductase context.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Enhanced formation of artemisinic alcohol/aldehyde and acid through supported P450 turnover
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Electron transfer support to CYP71AV1 system
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key auxiliary control factor for oxidation efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A. annua CYB5 was identified from trichome ESTs and expressed in yeast. 2. CYB5 increased artemisinic acid and aldehyde titres in a low-CPR1 background. 3. CYB5 is part of the full five-enzyme oxidation route from amorphadiene to artemisinic acid.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Single integrated copy expressed from GAL7 promoter in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical downstream enzyme to detoxify and pull flux through aldehyde oxidation to artemisinic acid. Should be coexpressed with ADH1 and CYP71AV1/CYB5/CPR1 for high titre production. Particularly valuable when toxic aldehyde intermediates accumulate.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes artemisinic aldehyde to artemisinic acid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Markedly increased artemisinic acid production, nearly eliminated aldehyde accumulation, improved ability to use early induction, and increased yield more than twofold versus initial strains.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast, metabolite profiling, fermentor performance comparisons, previous biochemical characterization cited
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction or constitutive GAL80-deleted context; early induction/repression fermentation; IPM extraction further improved apparent production
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not detailed here; identified from Artemisia annua
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: None newly generated here; prior isolation referenced
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ALDH1 expression led to extracellular crystalline artemisinic acid precipitate. High titre and improved viability/yield strongly support its importance in native plant biosynthesis as well.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Product precipitation complicated sampling; no major biological penalty reported, rather improved process robustness.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant and major bottleneck-relief step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ALDH1 catalyses the final oxidation from artemisinic aldehyde to artemisinic acid. 2. Introducing ALDH1 sharply increased acid titre and removed toxic aldehyde accumulation. 3. ALDH1 enabled early induction and was essential for high-yield industrial production strains.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Extracellular broth precipitate and IPM phase in engineered yeast

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Single integrated copy expressed from GAL7 promoter in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Add ADH1 with ALDH1 to complete oxidation pathway and remove artemisinic alcohol accumulation. Strong candidate for pathway completion in microbial production platforms; stacking with ALDH1 is recommended.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes artemisinic alcohol to artemisinic aldehyde in an NAD-dependent reaction
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Eliminated detectable artemisinic alcohol in flask cultures and increased artemisinic acid production by 18%; improved fermentor titre and pathway completeness.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Trichome EST identification, recombinant protein purification from E. coli, in vitro enzyme assay and kinetics, heterologous expression in yeast, metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms the immediate precursor to target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction/constitutive GAL80-deleted background; enzyme assays at pH 8.5 with NAD preference
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strong in glandular trichomes; 2.2% of ESTs in trichome-minus-flower-bud collection, 1.3% in glandular trichome EST collection, low in flower buds; comparable to CYP71AV1 across tissues with negligible expression in cotyledons and mature leaf trichomes
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: EST library data including NCBI accession 35608; 454 EST sequencing referenced
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ADH1 belongs to the medium-chain dehydrogenase/reductase superfamily; Km for artemisinic alcohol 11 ± 3 µM and kcat 41 ± 5 s−1; showed much lower activity with dihydroartemisinic alcohol and strong preference for NAD over NADP.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No explicit penalty reported; requires ALDH1 downstream to avoid aldehyde toxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Medium-chain dehydrogenase/reductase (MDR) superfamily; NAD-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important pathway-completion step; not explicitly termed rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADH1 was identified from trichome ESTs and biochemically shown to prefer artemisinic alcohol. 2. ADH1 converts artemisinic alcohol to artemisinic aldehyde, filling a missing step in the pathway. 3. Co-expression of ADH1 with ALDH1 and the P450 module increased artemisinic acid titre and eliminated alcohol accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Overexpressed in green pathway module in engineered yeast; exact promoter not specified here
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Widely useful precursor-supply engineering target for terpene production. Truncated HMG1 boosts mevalonate flux and should be stacked with branch-pathway enzymes and competitor suppression.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces HMG-CoA to mevalonate; truncated form increases cytosolic precursor flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports increased FPP supply for amorphadiene/artemisinic acid production in engineered yeast platform.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced platform engineering from prior strain construction; pathway design context in figure
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction system for overexpressed genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Shown in pathway overview as part of prior overexpressed precursor-supply module; not newly interrogated experimentally in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Common tradeoffs in sterol/isoprenoid engineering not discussed here.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Commonly treated as rate-limiting precursor-supply step; not directly tested here
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum-associated; truncated catalytic domain more cytosol-exposed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. tHMG1 is part of the engineered precursor-supply backbone for sesquiterpene production. 2. It increases mevalonate-pathway flux to support amorphadiene formation. 3. Although not the focus of this paper, it underpins the high-producing artemisinic acid chassis.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Gene deletion of GAL80; GAL-regulated promoters became constitutively active without added galactose in Y1284
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Delete GAL80 to remove galactose requirement and constitutively express GAL-controlled pathway genes in industrial fermentation. Useful for lowering process cost and simplifying feeding, but should be deployed only after pathway toxicity is mitigated.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GAL80 deletion enabled constitutive expression of all heterologous GAL-regulated enzymes and produced higher artemisinic acid titres than the inducible parent without requiring galactose.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion/replacement, strain comparison in flask and fed-batch fermentations
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as a major process-level switch controlling coordinated expression of the entire heterologous pathway; its removal improved production and eliminated inducer dependence once cell viability was sufficient.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose absence/presence; early induction/repression fermentations; improved viability context due to catalase and pathway completion
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Deleted in engineered production strain Y1284
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Native yeast galactose-response repressor; no pattern reported
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of GAL regulatory circuitry controlling inducible expression; deletion relieves repression
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional control of heterologous artemisinic acid biosynthetic genes under GAL promoters
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Strategy was used previously in amorphadiene-producing strains and only became practical here after improved viability of the full-pathway strain.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive high pathway expression could worsen toxicity in insufficiently optimized backgrounds; no specific penalty reported in Y1284.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: GAL7-driven ADH1, ALDH1, CYB5 and other GAL-regulated heterologous pathway genes including ADS and CYP71AV1
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts within native yeast GAL regulatory complex; specific complex details not examined here
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional repression relieved by gene deletion
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Deletion derepresses GAL-controlled pathway genes and increases their constitutive expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GAL80 deletion removed the need for galactose induction of pathway genes. 2. The constitutive GAL80Δ strain Y1284 produced more artemisinic acid than its parent. 3. This regulatory rewiring simplified industrial fermentation and contributed to the 25 g l−1 process.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Galactose-responsive yeast GAL network; engineering deletion bypasses galactose input
